summary: I served in the Vietnam War as a Specialist Fourth Class in the United States Army. I was involved in combat operations in Vietnam and Cambodia, including search and destroy missions. The war changed me profoundly, especially after losing all my close friends in an ambush in Cambodia in 1970. My innocence ended there, and the decisions I made during the war shaped who I am today.

I was a Specialist Fourth Class in the United States Army during the Vietnam War. My role included being an RTO and later a Recon Sergeant, where I led search and destroy missions alongside infantry units. The Army was my family in the field; officers and soldiers were like brothers who would die for each other. I left active service in 1971, but the Army and Vietnam remain the defining parts of my life.

During my time in Vietnam, I carried a short-timers stick made from a 105 mm Howitzer shell casing. It was polished brass with the center removed, about that long, and I carried it as a sort of good luck charm. It symbolized my time in combat and the artillery support we often called in during missions.

I served as a Recon Sergeant and RTO in reconnaissance units during the Vietnam War. We operated in the bush with infantry, conducting search and destroy missions and calling in artillery and air strikes. Recon was dangerous work, but it was a brotherhood where trust and leadership meant everything.

I was on top of an Armored Personnel Carrier when I was hit by shrapnel in my left hand during an ambush in Cambodia. That hit knocked me off balance and probably saved my life, as the forward observer sitting across from me took multiple rounds and an RPG blast. The APC was a critical part of our mobility and protection in combat.

During an ambush in Cambodia, the forward observer near me took the brunt of a rocket-propelled grenade (RPG) attack. It was a brutal firefight, and the RPG blast blew his body apart, though he was still alive and screaming. The RPG was a deadly weapon that we had to face regularly in combat.

I wore several Saint Christopher medals around my neck during my time in Vietnam. Priests at different base camps blessed them, and after every firefight, I would find myself chewing one between my teeth without realizing it. Those medals were a small comfort and a symbol of protection in the chaos of war.

The rations we ate in Vietnam reminded me of those from World War II and Korea, specifically the C rations. The food was tough to stomach after a while, but it was what we had. I never experienced World War II firsthand, but the comparison was clear in the field.

Along with C rations, we also ate K rations during my deployment in Vietnam and Cambodia. These were standard military rations that sustained us when fresh food was unavailable. After eating them for so long, I often couldn't stomach anything else.

C rations were a staple for us in Vietnam, much like in World War II and Korea. We ate them constantly, and after a while, they made me sick if I tried to eat anything else. They were basic but essential for survival in the field.

We fought against the North Vietnamese Army during my time in Vietnam and Cambodia. They were relentless enemies, and we had to make split-second decisions to kill or be killed. I even knew a former NVA colonel who switched sides and worked with us as a soldier of fortune.

I had five days of R and R at Camp Love near Da Nang on the South China Sea. It was a welcome break from the bush, where I finally had a bed, sheets, a bath, and real food after almost a year. I swam in the ocean, watched movies, and enjoyed the comforts we rarely had in combat zones.

Camp Love was an R and R center near Da Nang where I spent five days recovering from the hardships of the bush. It had beds, clean sheets, and real food, which was a stark contrast to the conditions in the field. That time there was a rare chance to rest and regroup before heading back to combat.

Camp Love, where I had my R and R, was located on the South China Sea near Da Nang. The ocean was a refreshing sight after months in the jungle, and I took the chance to swim and relax. It was one of the few moments of peace during the war.

After my R and R in Da Nang, I flew back to Saigon before returning to the bush. Saigon was a major hub during the war, and it was part of the cycle of combat and rest that we endured. It was always a brief stop before heading back into the fight.

I traveled as far as eight miles outside Phnom Penh, the capital of Cambodia, during my missions. We operated in Cambodia as well as Vietnam, and Phnom Penh was a key location near our area of operations. Our work was constant and dangerous in those border regions.

While on R and R, we got to watch the movie Bullet from 1968 starring Steve McQueen. I had never seen it before, and it was a funny distraction from the war. My friend and I played cards and laughed, which was a rare moment of normalcy.

Steve McQueen starred in the movie Bullet, which I watched during my R and R in Vietnam. It was a light moment amid the war, and my friend and I enjoyed the film and some card games. It was a brief escape from the harsh realities we faced daily.

I remember being totally naked in a bathroom at Fort Carson, Colorado, after leaving the military. I had just gone civilian clothes and drove out the gate in my convertible, flipping off everyone on the way out. Fort Carson was where I transitioned back to civilian life after Vietnam.

Colorado was where Fort Carson is located, and it was the place I left active service in 1971. After the war, I had some wild moments there, like driving out of the base in a convertible and flipping off people. It marked the start of my difficult transition from soldier to civilian.

Although I left active service in 1971, the Vietnam War didn't officially end until April 1975. That year marked the fall of Saigon and the end of the conflict, but for me, my war ended when I left. Still, the memories and losses stayed with me long after.

After the war, I traveled extensively with a marionette show from Key West, Florida, all the way to International Falls, Minnesota. Key West was one of the starting points of my post-military life on the road, performing in schools and art groups. It was a way to keep moving and avoid being tied down.

Florida, especially Key West, was part of my post-war travels with a marionette show. I toured schools and colleges across the country starting from there. Florida was a place where I began trying to find a new life after Vietnam.

International Falls, Minnesota, was at the northern end of my travels with the marionette show after the war. I toured from Key West, Florida, all the way up there, performing in various venues. It was part of my restless search for purpose after leaving the Army.

Minnesota, including International Falls, was part of my extensive travels with a marionette show after Vietnam. I couldn't stay still and moved from place to place, performing wherever people wanted to pay. Minnesota was one of the many states I passed through trying to find my way.
